events.text: The patient is a 63-year-old female named Margaret Okonkwo. She is of West African descent, born in Nigeria, and has lived in the United States for 30 years. She is a retired civil engineer, nonsmoker, with a BMI of 28.4. Her past medical history is notable for well-controlled type 2 diabetes (on metformin), hypertension (on amlodipine), and chronic kidney disease stage 2 at baseline (eGFR 74 mL/min/1.73m2). She has no prior malignancy history. Family history is notable for a maternal aunt with renal cancer diagnosed in her 70s.

At age 63 years, the patient was diagnosed with stage IV (T3b N2 M1, AJCC 8th edition) clear cell renal cell carcinoma (RCC) of the right kidney, with sarcomatoid differentiation comprising approximately 20% of the tumor. ICDO site: C64.0 (right kidney). ICDO histology: 8310/3 (clear cell adenocarcinoma). Summary stage: distant. The primary tumor measured 11.4 cm in greatest dimension on cross-sectional imaging, with extension into the right renal vein. Regional retroperitoneal lymphadenopathy was identified (largest node 3.2 cm). Bilateral pulmonary nodules up to 1.8 cm were identified consistent with hematogenous metastases. IMDC risk classification: intermediate risk (1 factor: time from diagnosis to systemic therapy < 1 year). PD-L1 expression (combined positive score) was 12%. No bone or brain metastases at diagnosis. Confirmation: positive histology by CT-guided biopsy prior to surgery.

At age 63 years (initial workup), the patient had a CT chest/abdomen/pelvis with contrast, which showed cancer. A right renal mass measuring 11.4 x 9.8 x 10.2 cm arising from the upper and mid pole of the right kidney with heterogeneous enhancement, central necrosis, and extension into the right renal vein without inferior vena cava involvement. Enlarged retroperitoneal lymph nodes in the right para-aortic and aortocaval regions (largest 3.2 cm, short axis). Multiple bilateral pulmonary nodules: right lower lobe 1.8 cm, right upper lobe 0.9 cm, left lower lobe 0.7 cm, left upper lobe 0.5 cm, consistent with metastatic disease. No hepatic, adrenal, or osseous lesions identified. Impression: Large right renal mass with venous extension, retroperitoneal nodal involvement, and bilateral pulmonary metastases consistent with stage IV RCC.

At age 63 years (staging workup), the patient had a bone scan (technetium-99m MDP), which showed no cancer. No scintigraphic evidence of osseous metastatic disease. Incidental degenerative changes at lumbar spine and bilateral hips.

At age 63 years (staging workup), the patient had an MRI brain with and without gadolinium contrast, which showed no cancer. No intracranial metastatic lesions identified. Mild nonspecific white matter changes consistent with chronic small vessel ischemic disease.

At age 63 years, prior to cytoreductive nephrectomy, the patient underwent CT-guided core needle biopsy of the right renal mass. Pathology result: clear cell renal cell carcinoma (ccRCC), ISUP/WHO grade 3, with focal sarcomatoid differentiation (estimated 20% of sampled tissue). Immunohistochemistry: tumor cells positive for PAX8, CA-IX (diffuse membranous), vimentin, and CD10; negative for TFE3, TFEB, CK7, and CK20. Ki-67 proliferative index approximately 28%. Necrosis present. No rhabdoid differentiation identified. The sarcomatoid component showed loss of epithelial markers and diffuse vimentin positivity. Findings consistent with high-grade ccRCC with sarcomatoid features.

At age 63 years, the patient underwent open right radical nephrectomy (cytoreductive intent) with regional lymph node dissection. Operative findings: right renal mass 11.7 cm, densely adherent to the posterior peritoneum but no adjacent organ invasion. Right renal vein tumor thrombus present, resected en bloc. Five retroperitoneal lymph nodes sampled (right para-aortic and aortocaval). Estimated blood loss 850 mL; received 2 units packed red blood cells intraoperatively. The contralateral left kidney was assessed and appeared normal. Postoperative course complicated by prolonged ileus requiring 6 days of hospitalization. Creatinine peaked at 1.9 mg/dL (eGFR nadir 38 mL/min/1.73m2) postoperatively, consistent with acute-on-chronic kidney disease, which partially recovered to eGFR 52 mL/min/1.73m2 at 6-week follow-up.

At age 63 years, final surgical pathology from right radical nephrectomy specimen: right kidney, radical nephrectomy - clear cell renal cell carcinoma, ISUP/WHO grade 4, largest dimension 11.7 cm. Sarcomatoid differentiation: present, comprising 20% of tumor volume. Rhabdoid differentiation: absent. Coagulative tumor necrosis: extensive (>50%). Lymphovascular invasion: present (right renal vein tumor thrombus). Surgical margins: negative (Gerota's fascia intact). Adrenal gland: not resected (separate adrenal not involved). Lymph nodes: 3 of 5 nodes positive for metastatic clear cell RCC (right para-aortic: 2/3 positive, largest 3.0 cm; aortocaval: 1/2 positive). Pathologic stage: pT3b pN2 M1. Immunohistochemistry: PAX8+, CA-IX+ (diffuse), EMA+, vimentin+, CD10+. BAP1 loss of expression by IHC noted (suggestive of BAP1 mutation). PD-L1 (22C3 assay) CPS: 14.

At age 63 years, the patient had next-generation sequencing performed for clear cell renal cell carcinoma based on a specimen obtained from the primary right renal tumor (cytoreductive nephrectomy specimen). A comprehensive tumor/normal paired sequencing panel (500+ gene) was used. Actionable and clinically significant alterations identified: VHL p.R200* (nonsense mutation, pathogenic, loss of function; VAF 48%), BAP1 p.L572fs (frameshift deletion, pathogenic, loss of function; VAF 44%), PBRM1 splice site mutation c.1419+1G>A (pathogenic, loss of function; VAF 39%), SETD2 p.R1625H (likely pathogenic; VAF 31%), KDM5C p.Q690* (nonsense, hemizygous loss; VAF 52%), TP53 p.R248W (missense, gain-of-function; VAF 22% - subclonal, potentially enriched in sarcomatoid component), CDKN2A homozygous deletion (copy number loss), MET amplification (copy number 7.2; high level gain). Copy number alterations also included: chr3p loss (encompassing VHL, BAP1, PBRM1, SETD2 loci), chr14q loss. No KRAS, NRAS, BRAF, or EGFR mutations detected. No BRCA1/BRCA2 mutations. No microsatellite instability (MSI-stable). Tumor mutational burden: 6.4 mutations/Mb (intermediate). PD-L1 (28-8 assay) tumor proportion score: 8%. Germline analysis (paired normal): no germline VHL, FH, SDHA/B/C/D, or MET pathogenic variants identified.

At age 63 years (6 weeks postoperatively), the patient had an oncology office assessment at the medical oncology clinic. Performance status ECOG 1. She had recovered adequately from surgery. Creatinine 1.45 mg/dL, eGFR 52. Postoperative CT chest/abdomen/pelvis confirmed interval resection of the right renal mass and retroperitoneal nodes; pulmonary metastases stable (right lower lobe dominant nodule 1.7 cm, slight interval decrease possibly related to postoperative inflammatory changes vs. minimal response). No new sites of metastatic disease. Given stage IV disease with residual pulmonary metastases, BAP1 loss, sarcomatoid differentiation, and MET amplification, the patient was counseled on systemic therapy options. Discussed enrollment in a clinical trial (none available at the institution at the time). Decision made to initiate first-line combination ipilimumab plus nivolumab per CHECKMATE-214 data, given intermediate IMDC risk and sarcomatoid histology (data suggesting enhanced benefit in sarcomatoid ccRCC). Patient verbalized understanding of immune-related adverse event profile. Cancer present, no treatment yet initiated at this visit.

At age 63 years (approximately 8 weeks post-nephrectomy), the patient received first-line ipilimumab 1 mg/kg IV plus nivolumab 3 mg/kg IV every 3 weeks for 4 induction cycles, followed by nivolumab 480 mg IV every 4 weeks maintenance, administered with curative/palliative intent for metastatic clear cell RCC. The rationale for IO/IO combination included intermediate IMDC risk, sarcomatoid differentiation (20%), BAP1 loss (associated with IO benefit), and PD-L1 positivity (CPS 14).

At age 63 years (after 4 cycles of ipilimumab/nivolumab, approximately 3 months on therapy), the patient had a CT chest/abdomen/pelvis with contrast, which showed cancer with mixed response. Pulmonary metastases: right lower lobe nodule decreased from 1.7 cm to 0.9 cm (partial response); right upper lobe nodule decreased from 0.9 cm to 0.5 cm; left lower lobe nodule stable at 0.7 cm; left upper lobe nodule decreased from 0.5 cm to 0.3 cm. Retroperitoneal: no residual adenopathy above the level of resection; new small right pelvic sidewall lymph node 0.8 cm (indeterminate, concerning for new nodal metastasis). Overall assessment: mixed response - partial response in pulmonary disease, possible new nodal involvement. No hepatic, osseous, or brain lesions. RECIST 1.1 overall assessment: partial response (sum of target lesion diameters decreased 32%).

At age 63 years (cycle 3 of ipilimumab/nivolumab), the patient developed grade 2 immune-related dermatitis (pruritic maculopapular rash involving trunk and upper extremities, ~20% body surface area). Managed with topical triamcinolone 0.1% cream and oral hydroxyzine for pruritus. No systemic corticosteroids required. Ipilimumab/nivolumab continued without dose modification. Rash resolved to grade 1 within 3 weeks.

At age 63 years (cycle 4 of ipilimumab/nivolumab), the patient developed grade 2 immune-related hypothyroidism (TSH 18.4 mIU/L, free T4 0.6 ng/dL). Asymptomatic aside from mild fatigue. Initiated levothyroxine 50 mcg daily with subsequent dose titration to 75 mcg daily. Thyroid function normalized at 6-week recheck. No interruption of immunotherapy.

At age 63 years (approximately 6 months on nivolumab maintenance), the patient had an oncology office assessment. ECOG performance status 1. She reported mild fatigue and occasional mild arthralgias of bilateral wrists and fingers, grade 1. Thyroid function stable on levothyroxine 75 mcg. Blood glucose well controlled (HbA1c 7.2%). No significant gastrointestinal symptoms. Imaging from prior visit showed mixed response. Patient counseled on continued nivolumab. Repeat labs: CBC within normal limits; CMP with creatinine 1.52 mg/dL (eGFR 48); LFTs normal; TSH 2.1 mIU/L. Cancer present, with partial response in pulmonary disease.

At age 63 years (approximately 9 months on first-line ipilimumab/nivolumab), the patient had a CT chest/abdomen/pelvis with contrast, which showed cancer with progression. Pulmonary metastases: right lower lobe nodule increased from 0.9 cm to 2.4 cm; right upper lobe nodule increased from 0.5 cm to 1.2 cm; left lower lobe nodule increased from 0.7 cm to 1.5 cm; multiple new small bilateral pulmonary nodules up to 0.4 cm. Right pelvic sidewall lymph node increased from 0.8 cm to 1.9 cm. New right external iliac lymphadenopathy 1.4 cm. No hepatic or osseous lesions. No brain metastases (dedicated MRI brain obtained separately, negative). RECIST 1.1 assessment: progressive disease (sum of target lesion diameters increased 68% from nadir). Overall disease burden significantly increased. Impression: radiographic progressive disease on nivolumab maintenance after initial partial response.

At age 63 years (at time of progression confirmation), the patient had an MRI brain with and without gadolinium, which showed no cancer. No evidence of intracranial metastatic disease. Stable mild white matter changes.

At age 63 years (9 months after initiation of first-line IO), the patient was seen in clinic for disease progression confirmed on imaging. ECOG performance status 1. She was symptomatic with increasing dyspnea on exertion (walking more than one block) and a new dry cough, attributed to enlarging pulmonary metastases. She denied hemoptysis, chest pain, or bone pain. Physical exam: mild decreased breath sounds right base. Creatinine 1.55 mg/dL. Decision made to discontinue ipilimumab/nivolumab and transition to second-line cabozantinib, a multikinase inhibitor with activity against VEGFR2, MET, and AXL, with particular rationale given MET amplification identified on tumor NGS. Discussed goals of care: patient desires continued active treatment. Cabozantinib 60 mg orally daily to be initiated. Patient counseled on hand-foot syndrome, hypertension, diarrhea, fatigue, and hepatotoxicity risks. Levothyroxine continued. Cancer present, progressing.

At age 63 years (9 months after first-line initiation), the patient received second-line cabozantinib 60 mg orally daily, initiated for progressive metastatic clear cell RCC after failure of ipilimumab/nivolumab. The MET amplification identified on tumor NGS provided additional biologic rationale for cabozantinib selection over sunitinib or pazopanib.

At age 63 years (4 weeks after initiating cabozantinib), the patient developed grade 2 palmar-plantar erythrodysesthesia (hand-foot syndrome) involving bilateral palmar surfaces and bilateral plantar surfaces of the feet, with painful erythema, blistering of the heels bilaterally, and difficulty ambulating. Managed with dose reduction to cabozantinib 40 mg orally daily, intensive urea-based cream (40% urea) applied twice daily to affected areas, gel insoles, and avoidance of tight footwear. Referred to podiatry. Grade improved to grade 1 within 3 weeks after dose reduction.

At age 63 years (6 weeks after initiating cabozantinib), the patient developed grade 2 diarrhea (4-6 loose stools per day above baseline, without incontinence). Initiated loperamide 2 mg after each loose stool as needed (maximum 16 mg/day). Dietary counseling provided (low-residue, low-fat diet; adequate hydration). Stool cultures sent and negative for infectious etiology. Diarrhea improved to grade 1 (2-3 stools/day above baseline) within 2 weeks with loperamide. Cabozantinib continued at reduced dose of 40 mg daily. No electrolyte abnormalities.

At age 63 years (8 weeks after initiating cabozantinib), the patient developed grade 2 hypertension (blood pressure readings consistently 152-162/94-100 mmHg). Pre-existing amlodipine dose increased from 5 mg to 10 mg daily. Blood pressure subsequently controlled to 134/84 mmHg at follow-up. No dose modification of cabozantinib required for hypertension alone.

At age 64 years (3 months after initiating cabozantinib, 12 months from initial diagnosis), the patient had a CT chest/abdomen/pelvis with contrast, which showed cancer with stable disease. Pulmonary metastases: right lower lobe nodule decreased from 2.4 cm to 1.9 cm; right upper lobe nodule decreased from 1.2 cm to 0.9 cm; left lower lobe nodule decreased from 1.5 cm to 1.2 cm; the bilateral small pulmonary nodules stable or slightly decreased. Right pelvic sidewall lymph node decreased from 1.9 cm to 1.4 cm. Right external iliac lymphadenopathy decreased from 1.4 cm to 1.1 cm. RECIST 1.1 assessment: partial response (sum of target lesion diameters decreased 27% from pre-cabozantinib baseline). No new lesions. Impression: significant response to cabozantinib. Overall disease burden decreased.

At age 64 years (3 months on cabozantinib), the patient had an oncology office assessment. ECOG performance status 1. She reported improved dyspnea (now able to walk 2-3 blocks without significant shortness of breath). Hand-foot syndrome grade 1, well-controlled with urea cream. Diarrhea grade 1, managed with as-needed loperamide. Blood pressure well controlled on amlodipine 10 mg. Weight stable. Creatinine 1.60 mg/dL (eGFR 45), mildly worsened from pre-cabozantinib baseline, attributed to TKI-related nephrotoxicity and decreased renal reserve. No dose modification at this time; plan to recheck in 4 weeks. LFTs: AST 38 U/L (upper limit normal), ALT 42 U/L (grade 1 elevation), all others normal. Thyroid function stable. Imaging showing partial response. Cancer present, responding to cabozantinib.

At age 64 years (6 months after initiating cabozantinib, 15 months from initial diagnosis), the patient had a CT chest/abdomen/pelvis with contrast, which showed cancer with stable disease. Pulmonary metastases: right lower lobe nodule stable at 1.8 cm (slight fluctuation within measurement variability from 1.9 cm); right upper lobe nodule stable at 0.9 cm; left lower lobe nodule stable at 1.1 cm. Right pelvic sidewall lymph node stable at 1.3 cm. Right external iliac lymphadenopathy stable at 1.0 cm. No new lesions identified. RECIST 1.1 assessment: stable disease (neither sufficient shrinkage for partial response nor sufficient growth for progressive disease from nadir). Impression: stable disease on cabozantinib, maintaining prior response. No new sites of metastatic disease.

At age 64 years (6 months on cabozantinib), the patient had a multidisciplinary tumor board review and follow-up oncology visit. ECOG 1. Patient tolerating cabozantinib 40 mg daily with manageable toxicity profile. Hand-foot syndrome remains grade 1. Diarrhea grade 1, well-managed. Blood pressure controlled. She reports increased fatigue (grade 1-2), particularly in the afternoons. Hemoglobin 10.8 g/dL (grade 1 anemia, new from baseline - attributed to TKI effect and CKD). Ferritin, TIBC reviewed - mild iron deficiency component, initiated oral iron supplementation (ferrous sulfate 325 mg alternate days given CKD). Creatinine stable at 1.62 mg/dL. LFTs normalized. Plan: continue cabozantinib 40 mg daily, next restaging imaging in 3 months. Discussion at tumor board: MET amplification confirmed on NGS; continued cabozantinib preferred over switch to VEGFR-targeted agent. No clinical trial eligibility at current time. Patient remains interested in investigational options. Goals of care reaffirmed: disease control and quality of life. Cancer present, stable disease.

At age 64 years (9 months after initiating cabozantinib, 18 months from initial diagnosis), the patient had a CT chest/abdomen/pelvis with contrast, which showed cancer with stable disease. Pulmonary metastases: right lower lobe nodule decreased from 1.8 cm to 1.5 cm; right upper lobe nodule decreased from 0.9 cm to 0.7 cm; left lower lobe nodule stable at 1.1 cm. Right pelvic sidewall lymph node stable at 1.3 cm. Right external iliac lymphadenopathy slightly decreased from 1.0 cm to 0.8 cm. No new lesions. No adrenal, hepatic, or osseous lesions. Impression: continued stable disease with modest ongoing response in some lesions. Overall disease burden remains controlled on cabozantinib 40 mg daily.

At age 64 years (9 months on cabozantinib, 18 months from diagnosis), the patient had a routine oncology follow-up visit. ECOG performance status 1. She continues to tolerate cabozantinib 40 mg daily with acceptable toxicity. Hand-foot syndrome grade 1, continues urea-based cream. Diarrhea improved to sporadic grade 1 events (approximately 1-2 days per month requiring loperamide). Blood pressure well controlled. Fatigue grade 1 with adequate iron supplementation (hemoglobin improved to 11.4 g/dL). Creatinine stable at 1.58 mg/dL. TSH 2.4 mIU/L on levothyroxine 75 mcg. HbA1c 7.4%, diabetes well managed. She reports good quality of life, able to participate in light activities and visit with family. Psychosocial: meeting with oncology social worker monthly; patient has support from family. No palliative care referral yet, but discussed as available resource. Cancer present, stable disease, good disease control on cabozantinib. Plan: continue current regimen, repeat restaging CT in 3 months, annual MRI brain surveillance given previous brain-negative MRI at progression.

documents.text:
CT CHEST/ABDOMEN/PELVIS WITH INTRAVENOUS CONTRAST

CLINICAL INDICATION: 63-year-old female with metastatic clear cell renal cell carcinoma, right kidney, status post open right radical cytoreductive nephrectomy with regional lymph node dissection. Currently on ipilimumab plus nivolumab combination immunotherapy (4 induction cycles completed). Restaging evaluation for treatment response assessment. History of sarcomatoid differentiation and prior retroperitoneal nodal metastases. Known bilateral pulmonary metastases at baseline.

TECHNIQUE: Multidetector CT of the chest, abdomen, and pelvis was performed in the portal venous phase following intravenous administration of 100 mL of iohexol contrast (Omnipaque 350). Axial images obtained at 1.25 mm slice thickness with coronal and sagittal multiplanar reconstructions. Comparison is made to the prior CT chest/abdomen/pelvis obtained at initial staging and to the early post-operative restaging CT.

FINDINGS:

CHEST:

Lungs and Airways: The trachea and central airways are patent and midline. There is no endobronchial lesion. The lungs are well aerated bilaterally.

Pulmonary nodules are again identified, all significantly decreased in size compared to pre-treatment staging:
- Right lower lobe: previously dominant nodule, now measuring 0.9 cm in longest dimension (previously 1.7 cm on the post-operative restaging CT, and 1.8 cm at initial staging). This lesion demonstrates markedly reduced density and slightly irregular margins, features which may reflect treatment-related inflammatory change or central necrosis within a responding lesion. There is no cavitation.
- Right upper lobe: a solid nodule now measuring 0.5 cm (previously 0.9 cm). Decreased in size.
- Left lower lobe: a pleural-based nodule in the posterior basilar segment measuring 0.7 cm, essentially unchanged from prior examination (previously 0.7 cm at staging). No appreciable change in morphology or density.
- Left upper lobe: a small subsolid-appearing nodule now measuring approximately 0.3 cm (previously 0.5 cm). Decreased.
- No new pulmonary nodules are identified above the threshold of detection. No new ground-glass opacities or consolidation to suggest pneumonitis. No pleural effusion. No pneumothorax.

Mediastinum and Hila: Cardiac silhouette is within normal limits for technique. No mediastinal or hilar lymphadenopathy. No pericardial effusion. The thoracic aorta demonstrates no aneurysmal dilation. The thyroid is not imaged on this study.

Chest Wall and Soft Tissues: No suspicious chest wall lesion. No axillary adenopathy.

ABDOMEN:

Liver: The liver demonstrates homogeneous enhancement without focal hepatic lesion. No hypervascular or hypovascular lesion suspicious for hepatic metastasis. The hepatic veins and portal vein are patent. No biliary ductal dilatation.

Gallbladder and Biliary System: The gallbladder is present and unremarkable. No cholelithiasis. Common bile duct within normal caliber.

Spleen: Homogeneous splenic enhancement, normal in size at approximately 11.2 cm in craniocaudal dimension. No focal splenic lesion.

Pancreas: The pancreatic parenchyma enhances uniformly. No pancreatic mass or ductal dilatation.

Adrenal Glands: The left adrenal gland is unremarkable. The right adrenal gland is visualized in the expected location; no discrete adrenal mass or nodule identified. No suspicious adrenal lesion bilaterally.

Kidneys and Retroperitoneum: The right nephrectomy bed is noted, with expected post-surgical changes. A small amount of fibrous tissue and slight soft tissue stranding is present in the right retroperitoneum at the nephrectomy site, unchanged in appearance from the prior post-operative examination and consistent with expected post-surgical scarring. No evidence of local tumor recurrence at the surgical bed. No new retroperitoneal mass.

The left kidney is present and appears normal in size and morphology, with symmetric cortical enhancement and no hydronephrosis. No left renal mass.

Previously identified enlarged right para-aortic and aortocaval lymph nodes have been resected. The retroperitoneum above the level of the aortic bifurcation demonstrates no residual or recurrent lymphadenopathy. No pathologically enlarged nodes are identified in this region.

Other Abdominal Structures: The abdominal aorta is of normal caliber. No mesenteric or omental implants. No free fluid in the abdomen.

PELVIS:

Bladder: The urinary bladder is adequately distended and shows no mural thickening, filling defect, or mass.

Gynecologic Structures: The uterus and ovaries are atrophic and unremarkable in appearance for age. No adnexal mass.

Pelvic Lymph Nodes: A new rounded soft tissue nodule is identified along the right pelvic sidewall, anterior to the right iliac vessels at the level of the right common iliac bifurcation, measuring approximately 0.8 cm in short axis. This nodule was not present on prior examinations and demonstrates mild homogeneous enhancement. Its rounded morphology and interval development raise concern for a new nodal metastasis, though it remains within a size range that could represent a reactive node, and no central necrosis or extracapsular extension is appreciated. This finding warrants close attention on follow-up imaging.

The external and internal iliac nodal chains bilaterally demonstrate no additional enlarged or morphologically suspicious nodes. The inguinal lymph nodes are not enlarged.

Bony Pelvis and Musculoskeletal: No lytic or sclerotic osseous lesion suspicious for metastatic disease in the imaged portions of the axial and appendicular skeleton. Mild degenerative changes are again noted in the lumbar spine with endplate irregularity at L3-L4 and L4-L5. No pathologic compression deformity.

Soft Tissues: No soft tissue mass in the pelvis. No pelvic free fluid.

IMPRESSION:

1. Mixed treatment response to ipilimumab/nivolumab combination immunotherapy.

2. Pulmonary metastases: three of four previously identified bilateral pulmonary nodules show appreciable interval decrease in size, most notably the dominant right lower lobe lesion which has decreased from 1.7 cm to 0.9 cm. The left lower lobe nodule remains stable in size. Overall, the pulmonary metastatic burden is substantially reduced compared to the pre-treatment baseline, consistent with a meaningful treatment response in the lungs.

3. Retroperitoneal: The previously resected right para-aortic and aortocaval nodal stations show expected post-surgical changes without evidence of local recurrence or macroscopic residual disease at the nephrectomy bed.

4. New right pelvic sidewall lymph node measuring 0.8 cm in short axis, not present on prior studies. This finding is indeterminate — while small and without overtly malignant imaging features, its interval development in the setting of known metastatic renal cell carcinoma raises concern for a new nodal deposit. Clinical correlation and follow-up are warranted. PET-CT or biopsy may be considered for further characterization if clinically indicated.

5. No hepatic, adrenal, or osseous metastatic lesions identified.

6. No intracranial metastases (not assessed on this study; dedicated brain MRI recommended per institutional surveillance protocol).

Overall, the imaging pattern represents a heterogeneous response to immunotherapy: substantial improvement in the pulmonary compartment consistent with partial response to treatment, offset by a new and indeterminate right pelvic sidewall node that could represent early nodal progression or a false positive finding. Continued close surveillance is appropriate.

HEMATOLOGY/ONCOLOGY FOLLOW-UP NOTE
Medical Oncology — Genitourinary Oncology Service

ATTENDING: [Attending Oncologist]
FELLOW: [GU Oncology Fellow]

---

CHIEF COMPLAINT / REASON FOR VISIT:
Ms. Okonkwo is a 63-year-old woman with metastatic clear cell renal cell carcinoma (ccRCC), sarcomatoid differentiation, presenting for routine follow-up approximately 6 months into nivolumab maintenance therapy. This visit is for interval toxicity assessment, laboratory review, and discussion of most recent restaging imaging.

---

HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS:

Ms. Okonkwo has a well-documented history of stage IV (pT3b pN2 M1) clear cell RCC of the right kidney, initially presenting at age 63 with an 11.4 cm right renal mass, right renal vein tumor thrombus, retroperitoneal lymphadenopathy (largest node 3.2 cm), and bilateral pulmonary metastases. Tumor pathology demonstrated ISUP/WHO grade 4 ccRCC with 20% sarcomatoid differentiation, >50% coagulative necrosis, and positive lymphovascular invasion. Immunohistochemical studies confirmed BAP1 loss of expression by IHC. Tumor NGS identified VHL loss-of-function mutation, BAP1 frameshift deletion, PBRM1 splice site mutation, SETD2 missense mutation, KDM5C nonsense mutation, TP53 p.R248W (subclonal), CDKN2A homozygous deletion, and high-level MET amplification (copy number 7.2). Tumor PD-L1 CPS was 14% (22C3 assay). MSI-stable. TMB 6.4 mutations/Mb. She underwent open right radical nephrectomy with retroperitoneal lymph node dissection approximately 8 months ago, with cytoreductive intent. Final surgical pathology confirmed 3 of 5 lymph nodes positive. She recovered from surgery uneventfully aside from a postoperative acute-on-chronic kidney injury (creatinine peaked at 1.9 mg/dL, partially recovered to eGFR ~52 at 6-week post-op visit).

Given intermediate IMDC risk status, the presence of sarcomatoid differentiation, BAP1 loss, and PD-L1 positivity — all features associated with enhanced benefit from immune checkpoint blockade — she was initiated on first-line ipilimumab (Yervoy) 1 mg/kg plus nivolumab (Opdivo) 3 mg/kg IV every 3 weeks for 4 induction cycles. Therapy was complicated by grade 2 immune-related dermatitis during cycle 3, managed with topical triamcinolone and hydroxyzine without need for systemic steroids or treatment interruption, and grade 2 immune-related hypothyroidism after cycle 4 (TSH 18.4, free T4 0.6), for which levothyroxine was initiated and subsequently uptitrated to 75 mcg daily with normalization of thyroid function.

Following completion of the 4-cycle induction phase, she transitioned to flat-dose nivolumab 480 mg IV every 4 weeks as maintenance. Restaging CT chest/abdomen/pelvis obtained after the 4 induction cycles demonstrated a meaningful partial response in most of the pulmonary lesions — the right lower lobe dominant nodule had shrunk from 1.7 cm to 0.9 cm, the right upper lobe nodule from 0.9 to 0.5 cm, and the left upper lobe lesion from 0.5 to 0.3 cm. However, a new right pelvic sidewall lymph node measuring 0.8 cm was identified and was indeterminate (concerning for early nodal spread). Overall the disease burden in the chest appeared substantially improved.

She presents today approximately 6 months into nivolumab maintenance (approximately 9-10 months total on immunotherapy). She reports doing reasonably well overall. Her primary complaints are:

1. Persistent mild fatigue, rated 3/10 in severity, which she attributes to the overall cancer burden and ongoing therapy. She notes it does not significantly interfere with her daily activities but is bothersome. She has been able to perform light household activities and take short walks around her neighborhood.

2. Mild bilateral wrist and finger joint achiness, which she first noticed approximately 6 to 8 weeks ago. She describes it as a diffuse, non-migratory aching in the small joints of both hands and bilateral wrists, without significant swelling or warmth noted at home. She has not taken NSAIDs given her underlying CKD (eGFR in the low 50s post-nephrectomy). She does take acetaminophen 500 mg as needed with partial relief. She grades the arthralgias as grade 1 — present but not limiting function.

3. No rash currently (prior dermatitis has resolved); she continues to use a gentle fragrance-free moisturizer as preventive care.

4. Thyroid symptoms: she feels she is at her baseline level of energy and denies cold intolerance, constipation, or edema beyond her prior history. She has been adherent to levothyroxine 75 mcg daily, taking it on an empty stomach each morning.

5. No new respiratory symptoms beyond her baseline; she denies new or worsening dyspnea, cough, or chest tightness at this visit. She states she is breathing about the same as the last few months.

6. No gastrointestinal symptoms — bowel habits normal, no diarrhea or colitis.

7. No visual changes, headache, or neurological symptoms.

8. Blood glucose management: she has been adherent to metformin (held briefly peri-nephrectomy but restarted once renal function stabilized). She has been monitoring blood sugars at home; fasting glucose ranges 105-130 mg/dL.

She has been receiving her nivolumab infusions in our infusion center and tolerated the most recent infusion without acute infusion reactions. She denies any new medications started since last visit.

---

REVIEW OF SYSTEMS:

Constitutional: Mild fatigue grade 1-2 as above. No fevers, no night sweats, no unintentional weight loss (weight stable per her report and confirmed on today's scale).
Respiratory: No new dyspnea beyond baseline; no hemoptysis, no pleuritic chest pain.
Cardiovascular: No chest pain, no palpitations, no lower extremity edema.
Gastrointestinal: No nausea, vomiting, diarrhea, or abdominal pain. Normal bowel habits. No blood in stool.
Genitourinary: Urinary function intact. No hematuria. She is s/p right nephrectomy; voiding without difficulty.
Musculoskeletal: Bilateral wrist and finger arthralgias grade 1 as above. No joint swelling. No myalgias. No back pain.
Dermatologic: Prior maculopapular rash resolved. No current rash, blistering, or pruritus.
Neurologic: No headaches, no visual changes, no focal weakness or numbness.
Endocrine: On levothyroxine; denies symptoms of hypo- or hyperthyroidism at this visit.
Psychiatric: Reports mild anxiety related to cancer surveillance but is coping well with support from family and monthly social work visits.

---

PAST MEDICAL HISTORY / SURGICAL HISTORY: (see initial consult note for full detail)
- Stage IV clear cell RCC (right kidney), sarcomatoid differentiation 20%, pT3b pN2 M1
- Type 2 diabetes mellitus (well controlled on metformin)
- Hypertension (on amlodipine)
- Chronic kidney disease stage 3a post-nephrectomy (eGFR ~50-52 at last check)

2. Initiate second-line cabozantinib (Cabometyx) 60 mg orally once daily with food. Rationale for cabozantinib selection:
   - Tumor NGS demonstrates high-level MET amplification (copy number 7.2), providing strong biologic rationale for cabozantinib over alternative VEGFR-only inhibitors (sunitinib, pazopanib), as cabozantinib simultaneously targets VEGFR2, MET, and AXL
   - Published data (METEOR trial) and real-world experience support second-line cabozantinib after IO failure
   - Her renal function (eGFR 44) is acceptable for TKI initiation; no dose reduction required at this time based solely on renal function
   - She has not received prior VEGFR-targeted therapy (IO/IO first-line), making her naive to this class

3. Toxicity counseling for cabozantinib completed in detail today:
   - Hand-foot syndrome (palmar-plantar erythrodysesthesia): Preventive measures discussed - urea-based emollient cream (e.g., Eucerin, CeraVe) twice daily to palms and soles starting day 1; well-cushioned footwear; avoid tight-fitting shoes; minimize trauma to hands and feet
   - Hypertension: Blood pressure logs requested; given pre-existing hypertension, we anticipate possible worsening and have a low threshold to uptitrate amlodipine or add a second agent; she understands to call if home BP readings are consistently above 150/95
   - Diarrhea: Loperamide to be taken at the first sign of loose stools; dietary modifications (BRAT-adjacent diet, low-fat, low-fiber); adequate hydration; call clinic if more than 4 stools above baseline per day or if dehydration develops
   - Fatigue: Expected; counsel on energy conservation, light aerobic activity as tolerated
   - Hepatotoxicity: LFTs to be checked at 4-week follow-up; she should report right upper quadrant pain, jaundice, or dark urine
   - Mucositis/stomatitis: Oral hygiene, avoid alcohol-based mouthwashes
   - Nausea: Take medication with a light snack if needed; antiemetics prescribed (ondansetron 4 mg ODT PRN)
   - Wound healing: Cabozantinib to be held at least 28 days prior to any elective surgery; no planned procedures at this time

4. Drug interactions reviewed: No significant interactions with metformin, levothyroxine, or amlodipine. Reviewed grapefruit/grapefruit juice avoidance (CYP3A4 consideration). Reviewed antacid/PPI timing (take cabozantinib at least 2 hours before or 2 hours after any proton pump inhibitors or antacids if needed). Patient does not currently use PPIs.

5. Continue levothyroxine 75 mcg daily. Repeat TFTs in 6-8 weeks.

6. Continue metformin 1000 mg twice daily and amlodipine 5 mg daily (anticipate possible dose increase given cabozantinib-related hypertension risk; close BP monitoring as above).

7. Follow-up labs in 4 weeks: CBC, CMP (including creatinine, LFTs), TSH, urinalysis. Phone or portal contact at 2 weeks to assess early toxicity (particularly hand-foot syndrome and diarrhea) and blood pressure.

8. Restaging CT chest/abdomen/pelvis with contrast in approximately 3 months (after approximately 12 weeks on cabozantinib).

9. Goals of care: Briefly discussed. Patient remains committed to active treatment. She has a strong family support system and has been engaged with the oncology social worker. I introduced the concept of palliative care as a complementary service focused on symptom management and quality of life - she expressed openness to a future referral but declines formal referral today. We will revisit this at the next visit.

10. Clinical trial: Reviewed current open trials at our institution. There are no trials currently enrolling for second-line or later RCC that this patient would qualify for. We will continue to monitor for new trial openings and the patient understands she may be re-evaluated for trial eligibility at the time of any future progression.

Patient verbalized understanding of the plan. Written materials on cabozantinib provided. She has contact information for the triage nurse line and understands which symptoms warrant urgent communication. She will follow up in 4 weeks or sooner if needed.

HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS

Ms. Okonkwo is a 64-year-old woman with a history of stage IV clear cell renal cell carcinoma (ccRCC), right kidney, with sarcomatoid differentiation comprising approximately 20% of the tumor burden, presenting today for routine restaging and toxicity assessment on cabozantinib 40 mg orally daily.

Brief oncologic history: She was diagnosed approximately 12 months ago with a large right renal mass (11.4 cm on staging CT) with involvement of the right renal vein, retroperitoneal lymphadenopathy, and bilateral pulmonary nodules consistent with hematogenous metastases, establishing stage IV (pT3b pN2 M1) disease at presentation. She underwent open right radical nephrectomy with regional lymphadenectomy with cytoreductive intent, and final surgical pathology confirmed ISUP/WHO grade 4 ccRCC, 20% sarcomatoid differentiation, extensive coagulative necrosis (>50%), and 3 of 5 lymph nodes positive. Tumor genomic profiling by comprehensive paired tumor-normal NGS revealed VHL loss-of-function mutation, BAP1 frameshift deletion (with loss of BAP1 protein expression by IHC), PBRM1 splice-site loss-of-function mutation, SETD2 p.R1625H, KDM5C truncating mutation, TP53 p.R248W (subclonal, enriched in sarcomatoid component), CDKN2A homozygous deletion, and high-level MET amplification (copy number 7.2). TMB 6.4 mut/Mb; MSS; PD-L1 CPS 14.

Given intermediate IMDC risk classification, sarcomatoid histology, and favorable immunologic features (BAP1 loss, PD-L1 positivity), she was initiated on first-line ipilimumab (Yervoy) 1 mg/kg + nivolumab (Opdivo) 3 mg/kg IV every 3 weeks for 4 induction cycles, followed by nivolumab 480 mg IV every 4 weeks maintenance, per CheckMate-214 protocol. Treatment was complicated by grade 2 immune-related dermatitis (cycle 3, managed with topical triamcinolone without systemic steroids), and grade 2 immune-mediated hypothyroidism (cycle 4, asymptomatic, initiated on levothyroxine, dose subsequently titrated to 75 mcg daily). After 4 induction cycles she demonstrated a partial response in her pulmonary metastases. Unfortunately, approximately 9 months into therapy, repeat restaging CT demonstrated radiographic disease progression with significant enlargement of pulmonary nodules (right lower lobe dominant lesion from 0.9 cm to 2.4 cm), new bilateral pulmonary nodules, and progression of right pelvic sidewall and new right external iliac lymphadenopathy. An MRI brain obtained at the time of progression showed no intracranial metastases. She was symptomatic at that time with increased dyspnea on exertion and new dry cough.

Given progression through checkpoint blockade, combined with the finding of high-level MET amplification on tumor NGS, she was transitioned to cabozantinib (Cabometyx), a multikinase inhibitor with potent activity against VEGFR2, MET, and AXL, at 60 mg orally daily. Over the first several weeks of therapy, she experienced grade 2 palmar-plantar erythrodysesthesia (bilateral palmar and plantar surfaces with blistering and difficulty ambulating), grade 2 diarrhea (4-6 loose stools per day above baseline), and grade 2 hypertension (BPs 152-162/94-100 mmHg). The hand-foot syndrome and diarrhea prompted dose reduction to 40 mg orally daily, which was well-tolerated. Hypertension was managed by increasing amlodipine from 5 mg to 10 mg daily with good subsequent blood pressure control. The podiatry service was also consulted.

She returns today for her 3-month restaging visit. Overall, she feels notably better compared to the time of progression. She specifically endorses significant improvement in her dyspnea; she can now walk 2 to 3 blocks on a flat surface without stopping, compared to limiting breathlessness with less than one block of ambulation at the time cabozantinib was initiated. Her dry cough has also improved markedly. She denies hemoptysis, chest pain, fever, chills, or new bone pain. She reports persistent but mild discomfort in the soles of her feet, predominantly at the heels, which she manages with thick-soled gel insoles and application of 40% urea cream (Keralac) twice daily. She reports on average 2 to 3 looser stools per day above her baseline, which is controllable and not significantly disruptive. She does not require loperamide on a daily basis at this point. She denies any mucositis, nausea, or vomiting.

Her fatigue is present but manageable -- she describes it as a moderate afternoon fatigue, typically a 4-5 out of 10 in severity. She is able to perform most activities of daily living independently. She notes mild bilateral wrist and finger achiness, a residual complaint from her time on nivolumab maintenance that has slightly persisted; she manages with acetaminophen as needed (no NSAIDs given CKD). Her blood sugars at home have been in the 110-130 mg/dL fasting range. She is checking her blood pressure at home with a cuff; most readings in the 128-138/78-86 mmHg range on amlodipine 10 mg.

No dose re-escalation at this time given she has achieved good disease control at 40 mg with manageable toxicity; dose re-escalation to 60 mg would risk exacerbating hand-foot syndrome and diarrhea without clear added benefit, especially in the context of her CKD.

HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS:
Ms. Okonkwo is a 64-year-old woman of West African descent, retired civil engineer, with a history of stage IV clear cell RCC (right kidney, pT3b pN2 M1) featuring 20% sarcomatoid differentiation and BAP1 loss. She was initially diagnosed approximately 18 months ago with a large right renal mass (11.4 cm) with extension into the right renal vein, retroperitoneal lymphadenopathy, and bilateral pulmonary metastases. She underwent open right radical nephrectomy with regional lymph node dissection (cytoreductive intent) followed by first-line combination ipilimumab plus nivolumab (Yervoy/Opdivo) per CHECKMATE-214. She achieved an initial partial response in her pulmonary disease but developed radiographic progression at approximately 9 months on therapy, with enlarging pulmonary nodules, an enlarging right pelvic sidewall lymph node, and new right external iliac adenopathy. Tumor NGS from the nephrectomy specimen had identified a MET amplification (copy number 7.2) along with VHL loss, BAP1 frameshift, PBRM1 splice site mutation, SETD2 mutation, and subclonal TP53 R248W. Based on this molecular landscape - and in particular the MET amplification - she was transitioned to second-line cabozantinib (Cabometyx) 60 mg daily.

Cabozantinib was initiated approximately 6 months ago. She was dose-reduced to 40 mg daily at week 4 due to grade 2 palmar-plantar erythrodysesthesia (hand-foot syndrome), with good response to dose reduction and urea-based cream. She also developed grade 2 diarrhea at week 6, managed with loperamide and dietary counseling, improving to grade 1. Grade 2 hypertension emerged at week 8; amlodipine was uptitrated from 5 mg to 10 mg daily with adequate blood pressure control thereafter. Restaging CT approximately 3 months into cabozantinib showed meaningful shrinkage of her pulmonary and pelvic nodal disease (roughly 27% reduction in total tumor burden from the pre-cabozantinib baseline). The most recent restaging CT at 6 months (reviewed today and presented at tumor board) shows stable disease - her pulmonary nodules are essentially unchanged (right lower lobe 1.8 cm, right upper lobe 0.9 cm, left lower lobe 1.1 cm), and her right pelvic sidewall and external iliac nodes are stable at 1.3 cm and 1.0 cm respectively. There are no new sites of disease.

She reports her energy has been somewhat lower than usual, particularly in the afternoons. She attributed this to her anemia, which was first noted at the 3-month visit. She is taking ferrous sulfate 325 mg on alternate days as initiated previously (alternate-day dosing selected given her CKD stage 3A). She denies worsening hand or foot pain. She reports 1-2 episodes per month of looser stools requiring loperamide, which she considers well within an acceptable quality-of-life range. She denies nausea, vomiting, abdominal pain, mucositis, weight loss, or fevers. Blood pressure readings at home have been running 130-138/82-88 mmHg on amlodipine 10 mg. She has not noticed worsening shortness of breath and is comfortable walking 2-3 blocks. She denies hemoptysis, chest pain, pleuritic symptoms, or bone pain. No headaches, vision changes, or neurological symptoms.

Her hypothyroidism, managed with levothyroxine 75 mcg daily following ipilimumab/nivolumab-induced immune-related thyroid dysfunction, continues to be well controlled. Diabetes management has been stable on metformin.

From a psychosocial standpoint, she continues to see the oncology social worker on a monthly basis and describes strong family support. She remains engaged and motivated, expressing continued interest in any investigational therapies that may become available.

Case was reviewed today at our multidisciplinary genitourinary tumor board. Consensus recommendation was to continue cabozantinib 40 mg daily given ongoing disease control, acceptable toxicity profile, and the additional biologic rationale provided by MET amplification. The tumor board discussed that switching to a pure VEGFR-targeted agent (e.g., sunitinib or pazopanib) would be a step backward given the demonstrated activity of cabozantinib in this molecularly enriched context. No active clinical trial options are currently enrolling patients with this profile at our institution, though the team will continue to monitor for appropriate trial opportunities.

CHIEF COMPLAINT

Routine oncology follow-up for metastatic clear cell renal cell carcinoma on cabozantinib. Patient presenting for scheduled restaging review and toxicity assessment at 9 months of cabozantinib therapy (approximately 18 months from initial diagnosis).

HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS

Ms. Okonkwo is a 64-year-old woman of West African descent with a well-established history of stage IV clear cell renal cell carcinoma (ccRCC) of the right kidney with sarcomatoid differentiation (20%), right renal vein tumor thrombus, retroperitoneal lymph node involvement (3 of 5 nodes), and bilateral pulmonary metastases at diagnosis. She is now approximately 18 months out from her initial diagnosis and 9 months into second-line treatment with cabozantinib 40 mg orally daily.

To briefly summarize her oncologic history: She was diagnosed with stage IV (pT3b pN2 M1) ccRCC approximately 18 months ago after presenting with hematuria and flank discomfort. Tumor measured 11.7 cm at surgical pathology with ISUP/WHO grade 4 disease, sarcomatoid dedifferentiation in approximately 20% of the specimen, extensive coagulative necrosis, and lymphovascular invasion via the right renal vein. BAP1 loss was confirmed by IHC; tumor NGS (500+ gene panel, tumor-normal paired) revealed VHL p.R200* (pathogenic), BAP1 p.L572fs (pathogenic), PBRM1 splice site mutation, SETD2 p.R1625H (likely pathogenic), KDM5C p.Q690* (hemizygous), subclonal TP53 p.R248W (VAF 22%), CDKN2A homozygous deletion, and notably high-level MET amplification (copy number 7.2). MSI-stable; TMB 6.4 mut/Mb; PD-L1 CPS 14%. She underwent cytoreductive open right radical nephrectomy with retroperitoneal lymph node dissection without perioperative complications aside from prolonged ileus and acute-on-chronic kidney injury (eGFR nadir 38, recovering to 52 at 6-week post-op visit). She then received first-line Yervoy 1 mg/kg plus Opdivo 3 mg/kg every 3 weeks for 4 induction cycles, followed by Opdivo 480 mg every 4 weeks maintenance, based on CHECKMATE-214 data and the well-established activity of IO/IO doublets in sarcomatoid ccRCC and BAP1-deficient tumors. She achieved a partial response in pulmonary disease after 4 induction cycles (dominant right lower lobe nodule 1.7 cm → 0.9 cm) but subsequently developed radiographic progression at approximately 9 months into this regimen, with enlargement of pulmonary nodules (right lower lobe up to 2.4 cm) and new pelvic sidewall and right external iliac lymphadenopathy. She had clinical symptoms of dyspnea on exertion and new dry cough at that time. An MRI brain at the time of progression was negative for intracranial disease. She was then transitioned to cabozantinib 60 mg daily as second-line therapy, with the MET amplification providing additional biologic rationale. The starting dose was subsequently reduced to 40 mg daily after 4 weeks due to grade 2 palmar-plantar erythrodysesthesia (bilateral palmar and plantar blistering, difficulty ambulating), which improved to grade 1 with dose reduction and intensive urea-based cream. She subsequently developed grade 2 diarrhea (4-6 loose stools/day) managed successfully with loperamide and dietary modification, and grade 2 hypertension managed with uptitration of amlodipine from 5 mg to 10 mg daily. The first restaging CT at 3 months on cabozantinib showed a meaningful response (right lower lobe nodule 2.4 cm → 1.9 cm; right pelvic sidewall node 1.9 cm → 1.4 cm; overall sum of target lesions decreased approximately 27% from pre-cabo baseline). Restaging at 6 months confirmed maintained stable disease with no new lesions. At the 6-month tumor board review, continued cabozantinib at 40 mg daily was endorsed given the MET amplification and absence of clinically available trial options; mild iron-deficiency anemia was identified (hemoglobin 10.8 g/dL) and oral ferrous sulfate alternate day dosing was initiated given her CKD. Today she presents for her 9-month restaging evaluation.

Regarding today's visit: Ms. Okonkwo reports overall feeling reasonably well. Her main ongoing concerns are fatigue and mild bilateral hand and foot sensitivity. She denies new or worsening shortness of breath and reports she is able to walk several blocks at a comfortable pace without significant dyspnea -- a meaningful improvement from the symptomatic progression she experienced on nivo maintenance approximately 9 months ago. She denies hemoptysis, chest pain, palpitations, or pleuritic pain. She denies bone pain, back pain, or new neurological symptoms such as headache, vision changes, or focal weakness. She has had no hematuria or new genitourinary symptoms from her remaining left kidney. Bowel habits are near-normal; she notes occasional loose stools (approximately 1-2 days per month) but is not requiring daily loperamide and this does not significantly impair her daily functioning. Appetite is preserved; weight is stable. She notes mild ankle edema at the end of the day, bilateral, which she has attributed to warm weather and prolonged sitting, though this will be examined. She denies any new skin changes, rash, or mucositis. She reports good energy in the mornings with afternoon fatigue that limits her to about 4-6 hours of productive activity daily. She continues to live at home independently with her husband, manages her own medications, and has been visiting with grandchildren, which she describes as motivating. She is continuing monthly meetings with the oncology social worker, which she finds supportive. She is adherent to cabozantinib 40 mg daily and takes her doses consistently in the evening with a light snack.

She denies missed doses of more than 1-2 days over the past 3 months (recalled once holding cabozantinib for 2 days around a family event due to gastrointestinal concerns, but otherwise consistent). She takes her levothyroxine in the morning, separated from cabozantinib by at least 4 hours, per prior counseling. She remains adherent to metformin 1000 mg twice daily and amlodipine 10 mg daily, and is taking ferrous sulfate 325 mg on alternate days. Given that Ms. Okonkwo is now 18 months from diagnosis with her disease remaining controlled on second-line cabozantinib, we have a meaningful window to consider what options might be available upon future progression. Her tumor molecular profile -- particularly the high-level MET amplification, CDKN2A deletion, and BAP1 loss -- may render her eligible for emerging clinical trials. We will continue monitoring the clinical trial landscape. At the time of her next progression, potential considerations would include enrollment in a trial evaluating MET-directed therapy, VEGFR/HIF-2alpha inhibitors (e.g., belzutifan given VHL loss), or combination strategies. NUCLEAR MEDICINE BONE SCINTIGRAPHY REPORT

CLINICAL INDICATION:
Margaret Okonkwo is a 63-year-old female with newly diagnosed right renal mass measuring 11.4 cm on recent CT imaging, with associated retroperitoneal lymphadenopathy and bilateral pulmonary nodules highly suspicious for metastatic disease. TECHNIQUE:
Whole-body planar bone scintigraphy was performed following intravenous administration of 23.0 mCi (851 MBq) of technetium-99m methylene diphosphonate (Tc-99m MDP). Anterior and posterior whole-body images were acquired at approximately 3 hours post-injection using a dual-head gamma camera with low-energy, high-resolution collimation. Spot views of the skull, cervical spine, thoracic spine, lumbar spine, pelvis, bilateral hips, and bilateral knees were obtained as clinically indicated. Single-photon emission computed tomography (SPECT/CT) of the lumbar spine and pelvis was performed for supplemental characterization of equivocal planar findings.

FINDINGS:

Skull and Calvarium:
No focal areas of abnormally increased or decreased tracer uptake are identified throughout the calvarium, skull base, or facial bones. Symmetric, homogeneous activity consistent with normal bony metabolism.

Cervical Spine:
Homogeneous radiotracer distribution throughout the cervical vertebral bodies and posterior elements. No discrete foci of abnormal uptake. Mild facet activity at C5-C6 and C6-C7 without discrete hot spots, felt most consistent with degenerative facet arthropathy in the context of age-appropriate changes. No scintigraphic features to suggest metastatic involvement.

Thoracic Spine:
Symmetric, evenly distributed tracer activity along the thoracic spine. No focal photopenic defects or areas of disproportionately increased uptake to suggest blastic or lytic osseous involvement. The posterior elements and costovertebral junctions demonstrate normal physiologic distribution.

Ribs:
No rib foci are identified that would raise concern for metastatic involvement. Several foci of mildly increased activity noted along the left 6th and 7th ribs laterally in a linear, contiguous pattern, highly characteristic of prior rib fractures at various stages of healing or chronic costochondral changes — this pattern is not typical of metastatic disease. The remainder of the rib cage demonstrates symmetric tracer uptake bilaterally.

Lumbar Spine:
Focal areas of mildly increased activity at L3-L4 and L4-L5 endplates and facet joints bilaterally. SPECT/CT correlation demonstrates these areas correspond to moderate endplate sclerosis and osteophyte formation on the CT component, entirely consistent with degenerative spondylosis. No evidence of cortical destruction, soft tissue mass, or paravertebral involvement. Findings are attributed to degenerative disc and facet disease rather than metastatic disease.

Sacrum and Pelvis:
No discrete foci of abnormal tracer uptake are identified within the sacrum, iliac wings, acetabula, pubic symphysis, or ischial tuberosities. Physiologic symmetric uptake at the sacroiliac joints bilaterally. No scintigraphic features suggestive of osseous metastases in this region. SPECT/CT of the pelvis demonstrates no destructive or sclerotic lesions.

Bilateral Hips and Proximal Femora:
Moderately increased, symmetric periarticular tracer activity at both femoral heads and acetabula bilaterally. CT correlation within the SPECT acquisition demonstrates narrowing of the hip joint spaces bilaterally with subchondral sclerosis and marginal osteophytes, consistent with bilateral hip osteoarthritis. No periosteal reaction or focal destructive lesion identified. These findings are degenerative in etiology.

Distal Femora, Knees, Tibiae, Fibulae, and Feet:
Symmetric physiologic activity throughout the bilateral lower extremity long bones. Normal periarticular activity at the knee joints. No suspicious focal lesions identified in the visualized distal appendicular skeleton.

Humeri, Elbows, Forearms, Wrists, and Hands (Visualized on Whole-Body Images):

Expected physiologic radiotracer distribution in the kidneys and bladder at the time of imaging. Visualization of the left kidney with normal tracer excretion.

IMPRESSION:

2. Degenerative changes at the lumbar spine (L3-L5 level) and bilateral hip joints, as confirmed on SPECT/CT correlation — these findings are entirely consistent with osteoarthritis and spondylosis and are not felt to represent metastatic disease.

3. Linear rib activity at the left 6th and 7th ribs laterally, most consistent with prior trauma or chronic degenerative costochondral changes. No aggressive features on the CT component. Correlation with clinical history is suggested; if the patient has no known prior rib trauma, short-interval follow-up plain radiograph of the ribs may be obtained at clinical discretion, though metastatic etiology is considered unlikely given the pattern and CT appearance.

4. Prominent activity in the right renal bed corresponding to the known large right renal mass.

MRI BRAIN WITH AND WITHOUT GADOLINIUM CONTRAST

CLINICAL INDICATION:
Margaret Okonkwo is a 63-year-old female with a newly diagnosed large right renal mass measuring 11.4 cm with heterogeneous enhancement and extension into the right renal vein, retroperitoneal lymphadenopathy, and bilateral pulmonary nodules identified on recent CT chest/abdomen/pelvis. The overall constellation of findings is consistent with stage IV clear cell renal cell carcinoma. This MRI brain is obtained as part of the systemic staging evaluation prior to planned cytoreductive surgery and initiation of systemic therapy. History of type 2 diabetes and hypertension. Baseline creatinine 1.3 mg/dL (eGFR 74 mL/min/1.73m2). Gadolinium administered with renal function review documented.

TECHNIQUE:
Multisequence MRI of the brain performed on a 3.0 Tesla scanner without and with intravenous gadolinium-based contrast agent (gadobutrol 0.1 mmol/kg). Sequences obtained include: sagittal T1-weighted localizer; axial T2-weighted fast spin-echo; axial FLAIR (fluid-attenuated inversion recovery); axial diffusion-weighted imaging (DWI) with apparent diffusion coefficient (ADC) mapping; axial gradient recalled echo (GRE) susceptibility-weighted imaging (SWI); pre- and post-contrast axial T1-weighted spin-echo; post-contrast coronal and sagittal T1-weighted sequences. Slice thickness 5 mm for standard sequences; 1 mm isotropic 3D post-contrast T1 MPRAGE acquired and reformatted in axial, coronal, and sagittal planes for high-resolution evaluation of parenchymal and leptomeningeal enhancement. No prior brain MRI available for comparison.

FINDINGS:

SUPRATENTORIAL STRUCTURES:

Cerebral Cortex and Subcortical White Matter: No focal cortical or subcortical enhancing lesion is identified. No mass, mass effect, or sulcal effacement. No areas of restricted diffusion to suggest acute or subacute ischemic infarction. No hemorrhagic foci on susceptibility-weighted imaging. No cortical or subcortical nodules concerning for metastatic deposits.

Periventricular and Deep White Matter: There are scattered, punctate to small, confluent foci of T2/FLAIR hyperintensity in the subcortical and periventricular white matter of both cerebral hemispheres, most prominent in the bilateral frontal and parietal regions, with a few additional foci in the posterior parietal white matter bilaterally. These do not demonstrate corresponding enhancement, restricted diffusion, mass effect, or blooming on susceptibility sequences. The distribution and imaging characteristics are consistent with mild-to-moderate chronic small vessel ischemic changes (Fazekas grade 2), which are not unexpected in a patient of this age with long-standing hypertension and type 2 diabetes. No perilesional edema. No leptomeningeal involvement.

Basal Ganglia, Thalami, Internal Capsule, and Deep Gray Matter: Unremarkable. No signal abnormality, enhancement, or mass identified within the caudate nuclei, putamina, globi pallidi, thalami, or internal capsule.

Specimen labeled "right renal mass, core biopsy" is received in formalin, consisting of three tan-white to pale yellow core needle biopsy fragments, each measuring approximately 1.4 cm, 1.2 cm, and 0.9 cm in length and 0.2 cm in diameter. DIAGNOSIS
---------------------------------------------------------------------------

RIGHT KIDNEY, UPPER TO MID POLE MASS, CT-GUIDED CORE NEEDLE BIOPSY:

CLEAR CELL RENAL CELL CARCINOMA (ccRCC), ISUP/WHO GRADE 3, WITH FOCAL SARCOMATOID DIFFERENTIATION (see comment).

Key diagnostic features:
  - Nests and sheets of tumor cells with abundant clear cytoplasm, well-defined cell borders, and prominent delicate arborizing vasculature ("chicken-wire" sinusoidal vascular pattern)
  - Nuclei with prominent macronucleoli visible at 10x objective consistent with ISUP grade 3
  - Focal spindle cell proliferation with marked nuclear pleomorphism, coarse chromatin, and conspicuous nucleoli representing sarcomatoid differentiation, estimated to comprise approximately 20% of the sampled viable tumor tissue
  - Areas of coagulative tumor necrosis identified (estimated 25-30% of tissue)
  - Increased mitotic activity: up to 6 mitoses per 10 high-power fields in the sarcomatoid component; 2-3 per 10 HPF in the conventional clear cell component
  - No rhabdoid differentiation identified
  - No definitive lymphovascular invasion identified in biopsy cores (limited sampling)

COMMENT:
Sarcomatoid differentiation in renal cell carcinoma represents a high-grade transformation that can occur in any RCC subtype and is associated with aggressive clinical behavior and adverse prognosis. In this specimen, the sarcomatoid component transitions abruptly from the underlying clear cell carcinoma, supporting its derivation from the ccRCC. The estimated proportion (approximately 20%) is based on available biopsy material and may not be fully representative of the entire tumor volume. The treating team is advised that sarcomatoid differentiation, even at low percentages, carries prognostic significance and may influence systemic therapy selection.

Grade is assigned to the highest-grade component of the clear cell carcinoma (excluding sarcomatoid areas, which by definition represent grade 4 transformation per WHO 2022 guidelines; ISUP grade 3 applies to the conventional ccRCC component based on nucleolar prominence at 10x).

MICROSCOPIC DESCRIPTION
---------------------------------------------------------------------------

Histologic sections of the biopsy cores demonstrate a biphasic neoplasm. The predominant component (approximately 80% of viable tumor) consists of nests, alveoli, and solid sheets of polygonal cells with abundant optically clear to finely granular cytoplasm, well-defined cell membranes, and a rich network of thin-walled sinusoidal capillaries in a classic "chicken-wire" pattern. Nucleoli are readily identifiable at scanning to low magnification (10x objective) in the majority of tumor cells, consistent with ISUP/WHO grade 3. The cytoplasm is predominantly clear, reflecting glycogen and lipid content dissolved during tissue processing, with occasional eosinophilic cytoplasm in a minority of cells.

A second component comprising approximately 20% of sampled viable tumor consists of fascicles and sheets of elongated spindle-shaped cells with markedly irregular, hyperchromatic nuclei, prominent eosinophilic nucleoli, and a fibroblastic to myoid appearance. This sarcomatoid component shows an abrupt transition from the conventional clear cell areas without intervening gradation. Mitotic figures, including atypical forms, are increased in this component. No rhab doid cells (defined as cells with eccentric nuclei, prominent macronucleoli, and globular eosinophilic cytoplasmic inclusions) are identified.

Coagulative tumor necrosis is present, affecting an estimated 25-30% of the sampled tissue. Geographic pallor with karyorrhexis and ghost cell outlines is characteristic of coagulative-type necrosis. Necrosis is present within both the conventional and sarcomatoid components.

No convincing lymphovascular space invasion is identified in the biopsy cores, though this finding is limited by the nature of the sampling. No renal parenchyma or normal collecting structures are present in the submitted cores.

MICROSCOPIC DESCRIPTION:

Specimen A:
Histologic sections of the primary tumor confirm clear cell renal cell carcinoma. The predominant component (approximately 80% of viable tumor) consists of nests, alveolar arrays, and sheets of polygonal cells with abundant clear to lightly eosinophilic granular cytoplasm, well-defined cell borders, and delicate, branching sinusoidal vasculature — morphologically and architecturally consistent with conventional clear cell RCC. Nuclear atypia is marked, with nucleoli prominently visible at low-power magnification (10x objective) in the majority of cells, consistent with ISUP/WHO grade 4. Occasional multinucleated tumor giant cells are present. Mitotic figures are readily identifiable (average 6 per 10 high-power fields in most active areas). Geographic coagulative tumor necrosis is extensive, comprising greater than 50% of the sampled tumor area.

The sarcomatoid component (approximately 20% of viable tumor) is composed of highly pleomorphic, spindled-to-epithelioid cells arranged in fascicles and storiform patterns. These cells display marked nuclear pleomorphism, coarse chromatin, prominent nucleoli, and conspicuous mitotic activity (up to 10 per 10 high-power fields), including atypical forms. The sarcomatoid component transitions focally and abruptly from the clear cell component. Rhabdoid differentiation (cells with eccentric nuclei, prominent nucleoli, and hyaline cytoplasmic inclusions) is not identified after thorough sampling.

Tumor extension into perirenal adipose tissue and renal sinus fat is confirmed microscopically. The renal vein lumen contains adherent intraluminal tumor thrombus with direct vascular wall invasion (venous wall insinuation, not merely a floating thrombus). The Gerota's fascia is intact; the closest surgical margin is involved by perirenal fat invasion but the fascia itself is uninvolved. The ureteral margin is negative.

The non-neoplastic renal parenchyma shows moderate global glomerulosclerosis (approximately 25-30% of sampled glomeruli), interstitial fibrosis with tubular atrophy occupying approximately 20% of the cortical area, and mild arteriolar hyalinosis — changes consistent with hypertensive nephrosclerosis and/or diabetic nephropathy superimposed on surgical compression atrophy. No evidence of a concurrent second primary renal neoplasm.

Specimens B and C:
Histologic sections of lymph nodes confirm the presence of metastatic clear cell renal cell carcinoma. The metastatic deposits are composed of large, polygonal clear cells with prominent nucleoli arranged in nests with rich sinusoidal vasculature, morphologically identical to the primary tumor. Focal extranodal extension is identified in one of the right para-aortic nodes (Specimen B), with tumor cells extending less than 2 mm beyond the capsule into the perinodal fat. No sarcomatoid morphology is identified in the lymph node metastases.

CLINICAL SUMMARY OF FINDINGS
─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
Comprehensive tumor/normal paired sequencing of this patient's right renal primary tumor identified multiple somatic alterations consistent with the genomic landscape of high-grade, sarcomatoid clear cell renal cell carcinoma (ccRCC). Key findings include biallelic inactivation of VHL (hallmark of ccRCC), loss-of-function mutations in BAP1, PBRM1, and SETD2 (chromatin-remodeling tumor suppressors residing on chromosome 3p), hemizygous truncating mutation in KDM5C, a subclonal TP53 gain-of-function mutation likely enriched in the sarcomatoid component, homozygous deletion of CDKN2A, and high-level amplification of MET. These findings collectively indicate a genomically complex tumor with multiple pathogenic chromatin-remodeling alterations, aggressive features (sarcomatoid dedifferentiation, TP53 mutation, CDKN2A loss), and a potentially actionable MET amplification. Tumor mutational burden is intermediate and the tumor is microsatellite stable. Germline analysis of the paired normal specimen revealed no pathogenic variants in hereditary renal cancer predisposition genes. See detailed findings below.

SOURCE ALTERATIONS: SOMATIC (TUMOR-ONLY VARIANTS CONFIRMED BY PAIRED NORMAL SUBTRACTION)
─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

SECTION 1: PATHOGENIC / LIKELY PATHOGENIC SOMATIC MUTATIONS

1. VHL — p.Arg200* (c.598C>T)
   Gene Function: von Hippel-Lindau tumor suppressor; ubiquitin E3 ligase complex component targeting HIF-1alpha and HIF-2alpha for proteasomal degradation
   Variant Classification: Nonsense (stop-gain); pathogenic
   Variant Allele Frequency (VAF): 48%
   Zygosity (by copy number context): Hemizygous loss of function; second allele lost via chr3p arm-level deletion (see Copy Number Alterations, below)
   Functional Consequence: Predicted complete loss of function of VHL protein. VHL loss leads to constitutive activation of the HIF transcriptional program, driving upregulation of VEGF, PDGF, EPO, and other angiogenic/proliferative targets. This is the canonical molecular driver of clear cell RCC and is present in approximately 90% of ccRCC tumors.
   Clinical Significance: Pathogenic driver; biallelic inactivation confirmed. Supports histologic diagnosis of ccRCC. Consistent with diffuse membranous CA-IX immunoreactivity observed on IHC (CA-IX is a direct HIF transcriptional target). No direct targeted therapy available for VHL loss in approved agents, but HIF-2alpha inhibitors (e.g., belzutifan) represent an emerging therapeutic class in this genomic context.
   Germline Status: Not identified in paired normal (germline VHL analysis below).

2. BAP1 — p.Leu572fs (c.1712_1713delAA; frameshift deletion)
   Gene Function: BRCA1-associated protein 1; nuclear deubiquitinase; chromatin remodeler and tumor suppressor; involved in H2A deubiquitination and regulation of the Polycomb repressive complex
   Variant Classification: Frameshift deletion; pathogenic (predicted loss of function)
   Variant Allele Frequency (VAF): 44%
   Zygosity: Hemizygous; second allele lost via chr3p arm-level deletion
   Functional Consequence: Frameshift results in premature termination at codon 573 (p.Leu572Valfs*2), eliminating the catalytic ubiquitin carboxy-terminal hydrolase (UCH) domain. Consistent with IHC showing BAP1 loss of nuclear expression in tumor cells (as noted in surgical pathology report).
   Clinical Significance: Pathogenic driver; biallelic inactivation confirmed. BAP1-mutant ccRCC is associated with higher tumor grade, sarcomatoid features, more aggressive clinical behavior, and poorer prognosis. Importantly, BAP1 loss has been associated with enhanced sensitivity to immune checkpoint inhibitor therapy (IO) in multiple retrospective analyses and subgroup data from CheckMate 214 and IMmotion151. BAP1 loss may also predict sensitivity to EZH2 inhibitors (investigational in RCC) due to synthetic lethality mechanisms.
   Germline Status: Not identified in paired normal.

3. PBRM1 — splice site c.1419+1G>A (intron 14 donor splice site)
   Gene Function: Polybromo-1; SWI/SNF chromatin remodeling complex subunit (PBAF complex); tumor suppressor
   Variant Classification: Canonical splice site mutation; pathogenic (predicted loss of function via aberrant splicing/exon skipping)
   Variant Allele Frequency (VAF): 39%
   Zygosity: Hemizygous; second allele lost via chr3p arm-level deletion
   Functional Consequence: Disruption of the canonical GT splice donor at the intron 14 boundary is predicted to result in exon 14 skipping or intron 14 retention, leading to frameshift and premature truncation of PBRM1. In silico splice prediction tools (MaxEntScan, SpliceAI) assign high confidence (score > 0.90) to splicing disruption.
   Clinical Significance: PBRM1 is the second most commonly mutated gene in ccRCC (after VHL), with loss-of-function mutations in approximately 40-50% of tumors. Biallelic PBRM1 inactivation has been associated with differential responses to immune checkpoint inhibition versus VEGFR-TKI therapy; some data suggest PBRM1-mutant tumors may respond preferentially to IO, while other analyses show variable effects depending on co-mutation context. In this tumor, co-occurrence of PBRM1 loss with BAP1 loss is notable, as these are generally mutually exclusive events in the broader ccRCC population; co-occurrence suggests a more complex genomic subtype with potentially distinct biology.
   Germline Status: Not identified in paired normal.

4. SETD2 — p.Arg1625His (c.4874G>A)
   Gene Function: SET domain-containing protein 2; histone H3 lysine 36 trimethylase (H3K36me3); tumor suppressor; role in transcription fidelity and DNA mismatch repair
   Variant Classification: Missense; likely pathogenic
   Variant Allele Frequency (VAF): 31%
   Zygosity: Hemizygous; second allele lost via chr3p arm-level deletion
   Functional Consequence: p.Arg1625His affects a highly conserved residue within the catalytic SET domain. This substitution has been reported in multiple independent RCC cohorts (ClinVar, COSMIC RCC entries) and is predicted deleterious by multiple computational algorithms (SIFT: Deleterious; PolyPhen-2: Probably Damaging; CADD score 29.4). SETD2 loss of function impairs H3K36 trimethylation, leading to defects in transcription-coupled mismatch repair and aberrant RNA splicing, as well as downstream effects on replication stress.
   Clinical Significance: Likely pathogenic driver. SETD2 loss-of-function mutations occur in approximately 15-20% of ccRCC. Loss of H3K36me3 may confer microsatellite instability-like repair defects at a local level; however, this tumor remains MSI-stable at the global MSI assessment scale. SETD2 loss co-occurring with VHL, BAP1, and PBRM1 loss on chr3p represents a pattern of broad chr3p tumor suppressor inactivation, consistent with the copy number data.
   Germline Status: Not identified in paired normal.

5. KDM5C — p.Gln690* (c.2068C>T)
   Gene Function: Lysine demethylase 5C (also known as JARID1C); histone H3 lysine 4 trimethylase; X-linked chromatin regulator and tumor suppressor
   Variant Classification: Nonsense (stop-gain); pathogenic (predicted loss of function)
   Variant Allele Frequency (VAF): 52%
   Zygosity: Hemizygous (X-linked gene; patient is female; VAF of 52% consistent with hemizygous loss at the X locus; the remaining allele may be subject to X-inactivation)
   Functional Consequence: Premature stop codon at residue 690 results in truncation of the C-terminal PLU-1 domain, eliminating histone demethylase activity.
   Clinical Significance: KDM5C mutations occur in approximately 7-10% of ccRCC and have been reported as associated with the sarcomatoid/high-grade phenotype. As a chromatin modifier, KDM5C loss contributes to the overall epigenetic dysregulation characteristic of this tumor. Emerging data suggest KDM5C-mutant tumors may have increased sensitivity to mTOR pathway inhibitors and potentially to HDAC inhibitors, though this is investigational.
   Germline Status: Not identified in paired normal; consistent with somatic origin.

6. TP53 — p.Arg248Trp (c.742C>T)
   Gene Function: Tumor protein p53; master transcriptional regulator of DNA damage response, cell cycle arrest, and apoptosis
   Variant Classification: Missense; gain-of-function (GOF); oncogenic
   Variant Allele Frequency (VAF): 22% (subclonal)
   Zygosity: Subclonal; heterozygous; no co-occurring deletion at 17p13 detected in this clone
   Functional Consequence: p.Arg248Trp is one of the most well-characterized gain-of-function TP53 mutations in human cancer. The arginine 248 codon directly contacts DNA in the major groove; tryptophan substitution abolishes sequence-specific DNA binding and confers dominant-negative activity over wild-type p53. GOF TP53 mutants have been shown to interact with transcription factors (NF-Y, SREBP, ETS2) to transcriptionally activate pro-oncogenic programs.
   Clinical Significance: Subclonal TP53 GOF mutation, VAF 22%, below the clonal threshold. The subclonal distribution and co-occurrence with the known sarcomatoid component (20% of tumor volume) raises the possibility that this mutation is enriched in, or potentially restricted to, the sarcomatoid subclone, consistent with published data showing TP53 mutations are significantly enriched in sarcomatoid and rhabdoid dedifferentiation in RCC. This may reflect clonal evolution and selection during tumor progression toward dedifferentiation. The presence of a subclonal TP53 GOF mutation is associated with adverse prognosis and resistance to some therapies. No homologous recombination deficiency signatures were identified in association with this variant.
   Germline Status: Not identified in paired normal; confirmed somatic.
