SECTION 2: COPY NUMBER ALTERATIONS
─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

1. Chromosome 3p Arm-Level Loss (chr3p21.31 – chr3p14.2 encompassing the entire short arm)
   Type: Arm-level somatic deletion
   Estimated Copy Number: 1 copy (heterozygous deletion)
   Genes Affected (clinically relevant): VHL (3p25.3), BAP1 (3p21.1), PBRM1 (3p21.3), SETD2 (3p21.31)
   Significance: Chr3p deletion is the most common somatic copy number alteration in ccRCC, present in approximately 90% of cases. This deletion provides the requisite "second hit" for biallelic inactivation of VHL, BAP1, PBRM1, and SETD2, each of which harbors a somatic point mutation on the retained allele (as detailed above). Together, these alterations constitute a compound genomic inactivation of all four major 3p tumor suppressors.

2. CDKN2A — Homozygous Deletion (9p21.3)
   Type: Focal homozygous deletion
   Estimated Copy Number: 0 copies
   Genes Co-deleted: CDKN2B (p15) co-deleted; ARF locus (p14ARF, alternate reading frame of CDKN2A) also deleted
   Significance: Homozygous CDKN2A deletion results in complete absence of both p16INK4a (CDK4/6 inhibitor; RB pathway regulator) and p14ARF (MDM2 inhibitor; indirect p53 activator) protein expression. In the context of a subclonal TP53 GOF mutation, concurrent CDKN2A/ARF loss further impairs the p53/MDM2 and RB/E2F pathways, likely contributing to the high-grade and sarcomatoid phenotype. CDKN2A homozygous deletion in ccRCC is independently associated with inferior outcomes in multiple cohorts. May also confer sensitivity to CDK4/6 inhibitor combinations (investigational context).

3. MET — High-Level Amplification (7q31.2)
   Type: Focal high-level somatic amplification
   Estimated Copy Number: 7.2 (high-level gain, not meeting strict amplification threshold of ≥8 copies but considered high-level gain)
   Significance: MET (hepatocyte growth factor receptor; a receptor tyrosine kinase) amplification is an uncommon but clinically significant finding in RCC, occurring in approximately 2-5% of tumors. High-level MET copy number gain activates downstream signaling through the RAS/MAPK and PI3K/AKT/mTOR pathways, driving proliferation, invasion, and angiogenesis. Importantly, cabozantinib (XL184) is a multikinase inhibitor with potent activity against MET in addition to VEGFR2 and AXL, and preclinical and clinical data support enhanced efficacy of cabozantinib in MET-amplified/overexpressing RCC. This finding provides strong biologic rationale for consideration of cabozantinib as a preferred VEGFR/MET-targeted agent in this patient.

4. Chromosome 14q Loss (chr14q22.1 – chr14q32.33)
   Type: Arm-level somatic deletion
   Estimated Copy Number: 1 copy (heterozygous)
   Genes Affected (clinically relevant): HIF1A (14q23.2) is hemizygous; PSEN1 and other loci affected
   Significance: Chr14q loss is among the most frequent copy number alterations in ccRCC and has been associated with adverse prognosis in several large cohort analyses. Hemizygous loss of HIF1A on 14q may modulate the relative contribution of HIF-1alpha versus HIF-2alpha to the hypoxia-inducible transcriptional program, as HIF-2alpha (encoded on 2p21, copy number neutral in this tumor) is not deleted.

SECTION 10: INTERPRETIVE COMMENT
─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

This tumor harbors a genomic profile fully consistent with high-grade, aggressive clear cell renal cell carcinoma with sarcomatoid dedifferentiation. The co-occurrence of biallelic VHL, BAP1, PBRM1, and SETD2 inactivation — all driven by the chromosome 3p arm-level deletion providing the shared second hit — represents one of the most genomically complex chromatin-remodeling alteration landscapes observed in ccRCC. BAP1 and PBRM1 co-mutation, while rare (each typically mutually exclusive in the broader population), has been described in a small subset of aggressive tumors and may define a distinct high-risk molecular subtype.

Of particular clinical relevance:

(1) MET amplification (copy number 7.2) provides a compelling biologic rationale for preferential use of cabozantinib over other VEGFR-targeted agents (sunitinib, pazopanib, axitinib) in this patient, given cabozantinib's documented MET inhibitory activity. Retrospective analyses from the CABOSUN and METEOR trials have suggested MET-high tumors may derive differential benefit from cabozantinib.

(2) BAP1 loss, PD-L1 positivity (CPS 14 / TPS 8%), sarcomatoid differentiation (20%), and intermediate IMDC risk collectively support the use of IO-based combination first-line therapy (ipilimumab + nivolumab per CheckMate 214), with BAP1 loss being one of the most consistently reported molecular predictors of IO over TKI benefit in ccRCC.

(3) The subclonal TP53 p.Arg248Trp GOF mutation (VAF 22%) is likely enriched in the sarcomatoid subclone. Sarcomatoid dedifferentiation is independently associated with worse prognosis and may predict differential response to IO over TKI. The presence of this subclonal oncogenic driver underscores the biological aggressiveness of this tumor.

(4) CDKN2A homozygous deletion is an additional adverse prognostic marker and may contribute to resistance mechanisms. In the context of this overall profile, clinical trial enrollment should be considered at disease progression, particularly trials evaluating HIF-2alpha inhibitors, MET-directed therapies, CDK4/6 inhibitor combinations, or EZH2 inhibitors in the setting of BAP1/PBRM1/SETD2 co-mutation.

(5) The intermediate TMB (6.4 mut/Mb) and MSS status do not provide independent support for IO use but are not contraindications.

(6) No germline pathogenic variants identified. Genetic counseling referral recommended.

CHIEF COMPLAINT

Ms. Margaret Okonkwo is a 63-year-old woman of Nigerian descent with newly diagnosed stage IV clear cell renal cell carcinoma (ccRCC) presenting 6 weeks following open right radical nephrectomy (cytoreductive intent) for evaluation of systemic treatment options in the setting of residual metastatic pulmonary disease.

---

HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS

Ms. Okonkwo is a 63-year-old retired civil engineer with no prior malignancy history who initially presented with flank discomfort and was found to have a large right renal mass on cross-sectional imaging. CT chest/abdomen/pelvis demonstrated an 11.4 x 9.8 x 10.2 cm heterogeneously enhancing right renal mass arising from the upper and mid pole, with central necrosis, extension into the right renal vein (no IVC involvement), enlarged right para-aortic and aortocaval retroperitoneal lymph nodes (largest 3.2 cm short axis), and multiple bilateral pulmonary nodules up to 1.8 cm consistent with hematogenous metastases. Bone scan was negative for osseous disease. MRI brain with and without contrast was negative for intracranial metastases.

Pre-operative CT-guided core needle biopsy confirmed clear cell RCC, ISUP/WHO grade 3, with focal sarcomatoid differentiation estimated at approximately 20% of sampled tissue. Immunohistochemistry was positive for PAX8, CA-IX (diffuse membranous), vimentin, and CD10, with noted high Ki-67 proliferative index of approximately 28%.

She underwent open right radical nephrectomy with regional lymph node dissection approximately 6 weeks ago. The procedure was performed with cytoreductive intent in anticipation of subsequent systemic therapy. Operative findings included a 11.7 cm right renal mass densely adherent to the posterior peritoneum without frank adjacent organ invasion, and right renal vein tumor thrombus resected en bloc. Five retroperitoneal lymph nodes were sampled. Estimated blood loss was 850 mL; she received 2 units of packed red blood cells intraoperatively. Postoperative course was complicated by a prolonged ileus requiring a 6-day hospitalization, as well as acute-on-chronic kidney injury with creatinine peaking at 1.9 mg/dL (eGFR nadir approximately 38 mL/min/1.73m2), which has been recovering.

Final surgical pathology confirmed clear cell RCC, ISUP/WHO grade 4, with 20% sarcomatoid differentiation, extensive coagulative tumor necrosis (>50%), lymphovascular invasion in the form of right renal vein tumor thrombus, and 3 of 5 retroperitoneal lymph nodes positive for metastatic clear cell RCC (pathologic stage pT3b pN2 M1). Surgical margins were negative with Gerota's fascia intact. Notably, BAP1 loss of expression was identified by immunohistochemistry on the surgical specimen, and PD-L1 expression by 22C3 assay yielded a combined positive score (CPS) of 14.

Comprehensive next-generation sequencing (500+ gene tumor/normal paired panel) of the nephrectomy specimen was recently resulted and is reviewed in detail below. Key findings include VHL p.R200* (pathogenic, loss of function), BAP1 p.L572fs (pathogenic, loss of function), PBRM1 splice site mutation (pathogenic, loss of function), SETD2 p.R1625H (likely pathogenic), KDM5C p.Q690* (hemizygous loss), subclonal TP53 p.R248W (VAF 22%, potentially enriched in sarcomatoid component), CDKN2A homozygous deletion, and notably MET amplification (copy number 7.2, high-level gain). Chromosomal copy number changes included chr3p loss (encompassing VHL, BAP1, PBRM1, SETD2 loci) and chr14q loss. Tumor mutational burden was 6.4 mutations/Mb (intermediate). No MSI detected. Germline analysis confirmed no hereditary predisposition variants in VHL, FH, SDHA/B/C/D, or MET.

A postoperative restaging CT chest/abdomen/pelvis was obtained approximately 2 weeks ago. This confirmed interval resection of the right renal mass and retroperitoneal lymphadenopathy. The pulmonary nodules remain present, with the right lower lobe dominant nodule measuring 1.7 cm, a slight interval decrease from the 1.8 cm seen at staging -- this likely reflects minor measurement variability or possibly a small degree of postoperative inflammatory response, though a treatment effect cannot be fully excluded. No new sites of metastatic disease were identified. Overall, she has residual stage IV disease with bilateral pulmonary metastases.

Ms. Okonkwo presents today to discuss options for systemic therapy. She is 6 weeks post-surgery and reports feeling significantly better from a functional standpoint compared to the early postoperative period. She continues to have mild fatigue, which she attributes to the surgery and overall cancer diagnosis. She denies worsening dyspnea, cough, hemoptysis, chest pain, or bone pain. She denies fever, chills, or night sweats. Appetite has improved; she has regained approximately 3 pounds from her postoperative nadir. Bowel function has normalized following the prolonged ileus.

Her IMDC (International Metastatic RCC Database Consortium) risk classification is intermediate risk, with one qualifying adverse prognostic factor (time from diagnosis to systemic therapy less than one year).

ASSESSMENT AND PLAN

Ms. Okonkwo is a 63-year-old woman with stage IV metastatic clear cell renal cell carcinoma, characterized by several high-risk features: sarcomatoid differentiation (20%), ISUP/WHO grade 4 on surgical specimen, extensive tumor necrosis, lymphovascular invasion, regional nodal metastases (pN2), BAP1 loss, CDKN2A homozygous deletion, and subclonal TP53 mutation enriched in the sarcomatoid component. MET amplification is also present (copy number gain 7.2), which is a notable finding. Residual bilateral pulmonary metastases confirmed on postoperative restaging CT. Performance status is ECOG 1 following adequate recovery from cytoreductive nephrectomy. IMDC intermediate risk (1 factor). PD-L1 CPS 14% by 22C3 assay on surgical specimen.

**Problem 1: Stage IV metastatic clear cell RCC -- initiation of systemic therapy**

The patient has completed cytoreductive nephrectomy and has recovered adequately (eGFR stabilizing at 52, performance status 1, hemoglobin 11.9 g/dL and improving). She is now an appropriate candidate for initiation of first-line systemic therapy. Key factors informing treatment selection were discussed at length today:

- Disease biology: Sarcomatoid differentiation (20%) is associated with aggressive behavior but also with enhanced benefit from checkpoint immunotherapy, as demonstrated in the CheckMate 214 trial subgroup analyses and corroborated in the CheckMate 9ER and other trials. BAP1 loss by IHC (and confirmed at the genomic level by NGS showing BAP1 p.L572fs frameshift) is associated with aggressive ccRCC and has also been implicated as a potential biomarker of IO responsiveness. PD-L1 CPS 14% is positive, adding further biologic rationale for immunotherapy-based combination.

- MET amplification (copy number 7.2, high level): This is an unusual but clinically notable finding and may have implications for second-line therapy selection if needed, particularly favoring agents with MET inhibitory activity such as cabozantinib.

- Renal function: Current eGFR 52. Metformin will need to be held or used with caution if eGFR drops further; this is being managed with endocrinology/primary care co-management. The reduced renal reserve following nephrectomy is an important consideration for agent nephrotoxicity monitoring.

- Clinical trial: Enrollment in a clinical trial was strongly discussed and explored as preferred first option. Unfortunately, no open and appropriate clinical trial is available at our institution at this time. Patient was counseled that she may be eligible for trials in the future if disease progresses.

Treatment Decision: First-line combination immunotherapy with ipi (Yervoy) 1 mg/kg IV plus nivo (Opdivo) 3 mg/kg IV every 3 weeks for 4 induction cycles, followed by Opdivo 480 mg IV every 4 weeks as maintenance, per the CheckMate 214 regimen. Rationale:

1. CheckMate 214 established superior overall survival for ipi/nivo versus Sutent in intermediate- and poor-risk metastatic ccRCC (HR 0.68 at 5-year follow-up), with durable responses and potential for long-term disease control.
2. Sarcomatoid differentiation: The sarcomatoid subset analysis from CheckMate 214 showed particularly compelling outcomes with ipi/nivo (ORR approximately 56% in the sarcomatoid subgroup), which substantially informs the decision in this patient.
3. BAP1 loss and PD-L1 positivity (CPS 14%) further support the biological rationale for immunotherapy.
4. While IO/TKI combinations (e.g., pembro/axitinib, nivo/cabozantinib) are also viable frontline options, the ipi/nivo combination was preferred given the sarcomatoid biology and the particularly strong data in that subgroup, as well as the goal of avoiding upfront VEGFR-TKI toxicity in a patient with CKD and diabetes.
5. The MET amplification provides additional rationale for reserving cabozantinib (which has MET inhibitory activity) as a potential second-line option.

Patient Counseling: Extensive time was spent today counseling Ms. Okonkwo regarding the immune-related adverse event (irAE) profile of dual checkpoint blockade. Specifically discussed:
- Immune-related dermatitis / rash (common; usually manageable with topical steroids or short-course oral steroids)
- Immune-related colitis / diarrhea (potentially serious; importance of prompt reporting)
- Immune-related pneumonitis (less common but potentially severe)
- Immune-related hepatitis (monitoring via serial LFTs)
- Immune-mediated endocrinopathies: hypothyroidism, hypophysitis, adrenal insufficiency, type 1 diabetes (relevant given her underlying type 2 DM; she was counseled on the need for close glucose and thyroid monitoring)
- Immune-related nephritis (relevant given baseline CKD; monitoring with serial creatinine)
- Neurological irAEs (rare but serious)
- The importance of NOT taking steroids for unrelated conditions without first contacting the oncology team, given the potential to mask or complicate irAE recognition and management
- The management protocol for irAEs: grade 1 toxicities can often be managed symptomatically; grade 2 may require holding therapy and/or systemic steroids; grade 3-4 generally require permanent discontinuation and high-dose steroids.

Patient verbalized understanding of the expected benefits and potential risks of treatment. She asks specifically whether she will lose her hair (alopecia is not a prominent feature of IO therapy; explained) and whether she will experience nausea (less common with IO compared to chemotherapy; discussed). She expressed agreement to proceed with Yervoy/Opdivo. Written informed consent obtained and filed.

First treatment session to be scheduled within 2 weeks, pending insurance authorization and pre-treatment baseline thyroid function (TSH obtained today, 1.8 mIU/L, normal; establishes baseline). Pre-treatment cortisol and ACTH not routinely obtained at baseline per institutional protocol unless clinically indicated.

Plan:
- Initiate ipi 1 mg/kg IV + nivo 3 mg/kg IV q3 weeks x 4 cycles (induction), then nivo 480 mg IV q4 weeks (maintenance)
- Baseline labs prior to each infusion: CBC, CMP (including LFTs, creatinine), TSH (every other cycle), fasting glucose
- Educate patient on irAE recognition; provide oncology triage phone number and after-hours protocol
- Monitor blood glucose closely; coordinate with primary care physician
- Restaging CT chest/abdomen/pelvis after completion of 4 induction cycles (approximately 3 months)
- MRI brain at restaging if any neurological symptoms develop

**Problem 2: Chronic kidney disease (stage 3a, post-nephrectomy)**

EGFR stabilizing at 52 mL/min/1.73m2 following right radical nephrectomy. Urine protein/creatinine ratio mildly elevated at 0.28 but not nephrotic range. Metformin currently continued as eGFR remains above 45, however, this will be reassessed with each visit. Amlodipine and other nephrotoxic agents held under close monitoring. Will coordinate with nephrology if eGFR declines below 45 or if proteinuria worsens. Will monitor closely given that immunotherapy agents, particularly Opdivo, carry risk of immune-mediated nephritis.

**Problem 3: Type 2 diabetes mellitus**

HbA1c 7.1%, well-controlled on metformin. Given upcoming immunotherapy, the patient was counseled specifically that immune-mediated endocrinopathies including new-onset type 1 diabetes or worsening of existing diabetes can occur with checkpoint blockade. She is instructed to monitor home blood glucose more frequently during treatment and to report any new symptoms of hyperglycemia. Primary care physician to be notified of new treatment.

**Problem 4: Hypertension**

Blood pressure well-controlled today at 128/78 on amlodipine 5 mg daily. No dose changes required at this time. Plan to continue monitoring at each visit. Blood pressure monitoring is particularly important as VEGFR-TKI agents (if needed in the future) can cause significant hypertension; immunotherapy agents less commonly cause hypertension.

**Problem 5: Mild postoperative anemia**

Hemoglobin 11.9 g/dL, improved from postoperative nadir. Normocytic, consistent with anemia of inflammation / postoperative state. No transfusion required at this time. Will monitor at each visit. Iron studies were not checked today but will be ordered if hemoglobin declines or fails to further improve.

---

FOLLOW-UP

Patient will be seen in the infusion center for cycle 1 of Yervoy/Opdivo in approximately 2 weeks pending insurance authorization. She will be seen in the oncology clinic approximately 3 weeks after cycle 1 for a treatment check visit, or sooner if she develops any concerning symptoms. Routine oncology follow-up every 3 weeks during induction phase, then every 4 weeks during maintenance.

Time spent with patient: approximately 60 minutes, inclusive of examination, counseling, and treatment planning discussions.
